content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

April 2, 2007

Julie Vaissade-Elock
City of Arcata Planning Commission Member
2865 Woodland Court
Arcata, CA 95521

RE: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-07-040

Dear Ms. Vaissade-Elock:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTIONS

1. Do the Act's conflict of interest provisions bar you from participating in governmental decisions related to a project involving a developer who has hired your husband to perform engineering related consulting work?

2. Do the Act's conflict of interest provisions bar you from participating in governmental decisions related to a project involving an applicant who is a regular customer of a retail store, which you own?

CONCLUSIONS

1. Yes. The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit you from making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision related to a project involving a developer for which your husband provides consulting services, even when your husband is not working on the specific project coming before the planning commission.

___________________________

¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, Sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-07-040
Page No. 2

2. Yes. The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit you from making,
participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision directly involving a
regular customer of your retail store.

FACTS

You are currently a member of the City of Arcata's Planning Commission.
Arcata encompasses a small geographic area and has a small number of larger
developers. Your husband, an engineering consultant, occasionally works for these
developers. One of these developers has a project coming before the planning
commission at an upcoming meeting. While your husband will not be working on the
project coming before the planning commission, your husband has been hired to work on
one of the developer's other projects.

In addition to your husband's engineering consulting business, you also own a
retail store. One of your regular customers, who shops weekly at your store, will also
have projects coming before the planning commission.

In a telephone conversation on March 14, 2007, you stated that your husband is
the sole owner of his consulting business and that the developer will be paying your
husband approximately $1000 for the consulting work. You also stated that you are the
sole owner of the retail store and that the regular customer, who will be appearing before
the planning commission, will make purchases from your store exceeding $1000 in the 12
months prior to any decisions pertaining to the customer.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a
governmental decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the governmental decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the
public official's economic interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) The
Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an
individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given governmental decision, which
we apply to your question. (Regulation 18700(b)(1)-(8).)

Step One: Are you a "public official?"

The Act's conflict of interest provisions apply only to "public officials."
(Sections 87100, 87103; Regulation 18700(b)(1).) A "public official" is "every member,
officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency . . .."

File No. A-07-040
Page No. 3

(Section 82048.) As a member of the City of Arcata's Planning Commission, you are a
public official within the meaning of the Act.²

Step Two: Are you making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental
decision?

A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) A public official
"participates in a governmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his or her
position and without significant intervening substantive review, the official negotiates,
advises, or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker regarding the governmental
decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use his or her official
position to influence a decision if, for the purpose of influencing, the official contacts or
appears before any other officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency.
(Regulation 18702.3.) As a planning commissioner, you are making, participating in
making, and/or influencing government decisions.

Step Three: What are your "economic interests?"

There are six kinds of economic interests recognized under the Act. Those
pertinent to your account of the facts are the following:

Business Entity - A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in
which he or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more, or in which he or
she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management.
(Section 87103(a) and (d); Regulations 18703.1(a) and (b).) "Indirect investment" is
defined to include any investment or interest owned by the official's spouse. (Section
87103.)

Sources of Income - A public official has an economic interest in any source of
income which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision.
(Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.) "Income" is defined to include any community
property interest in the income of a spouse and a pro rata share of the income of any
business entity or trust in which the official (or his or her spouse) owns directly,
indirectly, or beneficially, a 10-percent or greater interest. (Section 82030(a).)

² If a public official's office is listed in Section 87200 ("87200 filers" include planning
commission members) and he or she has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then
he or she must: (1) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, verbally identify each type of economic
interest involved in the decision as well as details of the economic interest, as discussed in Regulation
18702.5(b)(1)(B), on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the
duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. For closed sessions, consent calendars, absences and
speaking as a member of the public regarding personal interests, special rules found in Regulation 18702.5,
subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. (Section 87105.)

File No. A-07-040
Page No. 4

Personal Financial Effect - A public official has an economic interest in his or
her personal finances. In particular, a government decision has a personal financial effect
on a public official if the decision will result in the personal expenses, income, assets, or
liabilities of the official increasing or decreasing. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.)

Engineering Consulting Business – You have stated that your husband is the sole
owner of the consulting business. Under the Act, you have an indirect investment in any
investment in the business made by your husband. Accordingly, if your husband has an
investment of $2,000 or more in this consulting business, you have an economic interest
in the business as a business entity. (Section 87103 (a).) In addition to the economic
interest in the consulting business as a business entity, you have an economic interest in
the business as a source of income if your community property share of the income
generated by the business, in the 12 months prior to the decision, is $500 or more.
(Section 87103(c).) Based on the facts you have provided, it appears that you have an
economic interest in the consulting business as a source of income.

Developer – You have told us that the developer will pay your husband
approximately $1000 for his services as an engineering consultant. From your account of
the facts, it appears that your community property share of the income from the
developer, in the 12 months prior to the decision, is $500 or more. Accordingly, we will
assume that you have an economic interest in the developer as a source of income.

Retail Store – From all indications, it appears that you have an investment of
$2,000 or more in your retail store and that you are a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or manager of the store. Accordingly, it appears that you have an economic
interest in the store as a business entity. (Section 87103 (a).) Moreover, you have an
economic interest in the store as a source of income if you receive $500 or more from the
store in the 12 months prior to any decision. (Section 87103(c).) From your account of
the facts, you also have an economic interest in the store as a source of income.

Regular Customer – You have advised us that a regular customer, who will have
projects coming before the planning commission, will make cumulative purchases from
your retail store exceeding $1000 in the 12 months prior to any decision pertaining to the
customer. Again, based upon your account of the facts, you have an economic interest in
this customer as a source of income.

Personal Finances – You also have an economic interest in your personal finances
and those of your immediate family. A governmental decision will have an effect on this
economic interest if the decision will result in your personal expenses, income, assets or
liabilities or those of your immediate family increasing or decreasing. (Regulation
18703.5.)

3 You have not provided us with sufficient facts to determine whether you have a disqualifying
conflict of interest in any one decision because of your economic interests in your husband's engineering
consulting business, your retail store, or your personal finances. Moreover, in light of our conclusions in

File No. A-07-040
Page No. 5

Step Four: Are your economic interest(s) directly or indirectly involved in the
decision?

Regulation 18704.1(a) states that a business entity or source of income is directly
involved in a decision before the official's agency when that business entity or source of
income, either directly or by an agent:

"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing
an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;

"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the
decision before the official or the official's agency. A person is the subject
of a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval,
denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or
contract with, the subject person."

Business entities and sources of income that are not directly involved in
governmental decisions are regarded as indirectly involved. (Regulation 18704.1(b).)
From the facts provided, you are seeking advice pertaining to decisions in which the
developer or your regular customer is directly involved in the governmental decisions.

Steps Five and Six: Will there be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
on your economic interest(s)?

Materiality

Having identified the economic interests involved, and determined whether each
interest is directly or indirectly involved in the decision at issue, it is necessary to identify
the materiality standard appropriate to each economic interest.

For economic interests in business entities directly involved in a decision,
including business entities which are a source of income to an official, the materiality
standard is given by Regulation 18705.1(b), which states, "the financial effects of a
government decision on a business entity which is directly involved in the government
decision is presumed to be material." For economic interests in persons who are directly
involved in a decision, the materiality standard is given at Regulation 18705.4(a), which
states, "Any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a person who is a source of
income to a public official, and who is directly involved in a decision before the official's
agency is deemed material."

terms of your economic interests in the developer and your regular customer as sources of income, it is
unnecessary for us to further consider your other economic interests. Accordingly, we conclude our
analysis of your economic interests in your husband's engineering consulting business, your retail store,
and your personal finances at this point.

File No. A-07-040
Page No. 6

Foreseeability

Once a public official has determined the materiality standards applicable to each
of his or her economic interests, the next step is determining whether it is "reasonably
foreseeable" that the standard will be met. A material financial effect on an economic
interest is "reasonably foreseeable" if it is substantially likely that one or more of the
materiality standards will be met as a result of the governmental decision. (Regulation
18706(a).) An effect need not be certain to be considered "reasonably foreseeable," but it
must be more than a mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

Considering any financial effect on your economic interests in the developer and
your regular customer are presumed to be material, however small the effect, it appears
that the financial effect of decisions will be reasonably foreseeable.

Steps Seven and Eight: Does this governmental decision come within any exception
to the conflict-of-interest rules?

Even if an official has a conflict of interest, disqualification is not required if the
governmental decision affects the public official's economic interests in a manner that is
indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will affect the public generally.
(Section 87103; Regulation 18707(a).) Section 87103.5 provides a specialized form of the
"public generally" exception applicable to sources of income to owners of retail business
entities.

Generally, Section 87103.5 states that retail customers of a business entity engaged
in retail sales of goods and services to the public will not be considered sources of income to
a public official owning 10% or more of the business entity if the retail customers comprise
a significant segment of the public generally, and the amount of income received by the
business entity from any specific customer is indistinguishable from the amount of income
received from its other retail customers.

Regulation 18707.5 sets out a two-pronged test interpreting Section 87103.5.
Both prongs must be satisfied for the exception to apply. First, the retail customers
comprise a significant segment of the public generally if the "retail customers of the
business entity are sufficient in number to equal 10 percent or more of the population or
households of the jurisdiction" or "number at least 10,000" in the 12 months prior to the
decision. (Regulation 18707.5(a)(1), (2).)

If this numerical threshold is met and the official's business entity is located in a
jurisdiction with a population of more than 10,000 or in a county with more than 350
retail businesses, the amount of income received by a business entity from a particular
customer is indistinguishable from the amount of income received from its other retail
customers if the amount spent by that customer is less than one-tenth of 1 percent of
the gross sales revenues of the business in the 12 months prior to the decision.

File No. A-07-040
Page No. 7

(Regulation 18707.5(b)(1).) If the business entity is located in a jurisdiction with a
population of 10,000 or less and in a county with fewer than 350 retail businesses, the
amount of income received by a business entity from a particular customer is
distinguishable from the amount of income received from its other retail customers if
the amount spent by that customer is less than 1 percent of the gross sales revenues of the
business in the 12 months prior to the decision. (Regulation 18707.5(b)(2).)

While we have provided the above explanation of the public generally exception
and enclosed a copy of Regulation 18707.5 for your review, we have not been provided
with sufficient facts to determine if the exception applies to your particular
circumstances. If you need additional assistance in determining the applicability of the
public generally exception, you should seek further advice providing all relevant facts.

Additionally, in certain rare circumstances, a public official may be called upon to
take part in a decision under the "legally required participation" exception despite the fact
that the official may have a disqualifying conflict of interest. This exception applies only in
certain very specific circumstances where the government agency would be paralyzed from
acting. (Section 87101; Regulation 18708.)

You have not presented any facts indicating that the "legally required
participation" exception applies to your circumstances, so we will not address this
exception further.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: Brian G. Lau
Counsel, Legal Division

governmental decisions related to a project involving a develope. *ho has hired your
husband to perform engineering related consulting work?
2. Do the Act's conflict of interest provisions bar you from participating in
governmental decisions related to a project involving an applicant who is a regular
customer of a retail store, which you own?

CONCLUSION: 1' Yes. The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit you from making,

embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 April 2, 2007 Julie Vaissade-Elock City of Arcata Planning Commission Member 2865 Woodland Court Arcata, CA 95521 RE: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-07-040 Dear Ms. Vaissade-Elock: This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ QUESTIONS 1. Do the Act's conflict of interest provisions bar you from participating in governmental decisions related to a project involving a developer who has hired your husband to perform engineering related consulting work? 2. Do the Act's conflict of interest provisions bar you from participating in governmental decisions related to a project involving an applicant who is a regular customer of a retail store, which you own? CONCLUSIONS 1. Yes. The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit you from making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision related to a project involving a developer for which your husband provides consulting services, even when your husband is not working on the specific project coming before the planning commission. ___________________________ ¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, Sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. File No. A-07-040 Page No. 2 2. Yes. The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit you from making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision directly involving a regular customer of your retail store. FACTS You are currently a member of the City of Arcata's Planning Commission. Arcata encompasses a small geographic area and has a small number of larger developers. Your husband, an engineering consultant, occasionally works for these developers. One of these developers has a project coming before the planning commission at an upcoming meeting. While your husband will not be working on the project coming before the planning commission, your husband has been hired to work on one of the developer's other projects. In addition to your husband's engineering consulting business, you also own a retail store. One of your regular customers, who shops weekly at your store, will also have projects coming before the planning commission. In a telephone conversation on March 14, 2007, you stated that your husband is the sole owner of his consulting business and that the developer will be paying your husband approximately $1000 for the consulting work. You also stated that you are the sole owner of the retail store and that the regular customer, who will be appearing before the planning commission, will make purchases from your store exceeding $1000 in the 12 months prior to any decisions pertaining to the customer. ANALYSIS Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that
